Round 1.headline: Automated scoring lied. Blank screen, 0.935 composite.
Round 1.description: Only GAD ran in round 1 (v1-v5). The auto-composite said 0.935 but the game was a blank screen. This round proved human review is non-negotiable.
Round 1.conclusion: Automated scoring without human review is dangerously misleading. Led to gate criteria design, human_review weight increase, build-must-render rule.

Round 2.headline: Three conditions, one surprise: bare beat GAD.
Round 2.description: First three-condition experiment with v2 requirements. Bare v2 shipped the most playable game at 0.50 human review while GAD v7 scored 0.30 despite perfect process metrics.
Round 2.conclusion: Bare workflow beats GAD on human review even at equal requirements. Process metrics do not predict output quality.

Round 3.headline: The freedom hypothesis: bare beat GAD on creative implementation.
Round 3.description: Same task, same v3 requirements, all three workflows. Bare v3 scored 0.70, emergent v2 scored 0.50, GAD v8 scored 0.20. Less framework = better output. That sent us back to redesign v4 around pressure, not features.
Round 3.conclusion: Freedom hypothesis established. Bare outperforms GAD monotonically. Led to v4 requirements redesign around pressure, eval preservation contract, brownfield eval mode.

Round 4.headline: Pressure-oriented v4: freedom hypothesis holds.
Round 4.description: Under v4's stricter pressure requirements, Bare + Emergent both shipped complete playable games. Emergent v4 achieved 0.805 with full skill ratcheting. GAD was API-interrupted before implementation. Convergent design evolution observed across all conditions.
Round 4.conclusion: Freedom hypothesis confirmed under stricter requirements. First full skill ratcheting cycle observed. Rubric scoring replaces single-score human review.

Round 5.headline: 19 builds deployed. Awaiting review.
Round 5.description: Round 5 builds are deployed but human review is pending. Play the builds in the Playable Archive above and check back for scored results.
